bids.reasoning: Since this is the very beginning of the negotiation, I will start by offering my most preferred values across all issues to set a strong anchor. This strategy guarantees my maximum potential utility configuration of 1.0 and allows me to see the opponent's counteroffer before making any concessions.

The negotiation is extremely early (t=0.02), and the opponent's threatening message and lowball offer are standard aggressive opening tactics, which I will treat as cheap talk. I will demonstrate a willingness to negotiate by making minor concessions on issueA and issueD (moving from valueE to valueD), but I will maintain my top values on my highest-weight issues (issueE and issueB) to ensure my overall utility remains very high. This signals flexibility while firmly rejecting their extreme position.

The time is very early (t=0.04), and the opponent is using aggressive cheap talk ("I will walk away") combined with a regressive offer on issue E. I will disregard the threats and focus on the numbers. To show movement, I will concede entirely on issue C (which has my lowest weight and gives me okay utility at value B) to match their consistent request for value B, while holding firm on my highest-priority issues (E, B, and D) to protect my overall utility.

The current time is very early (t=0.06), so I have ample time to negotiate despite the opponent's aggressive threats, which I recognize as empty cheap-talk tactics. I will maintain a high-utility posture for myself, standing firm on my highest-weight issues (E and D) while making a minor concession on lower-weight issues (A and B) to signal willingness to negotiate. The opponent clearly desires valueB for issueC, which I will continue to offer.

The negotiation is still in its very early stages (t=0.08), giving me little reason to succumb to the opponent's aggressive messages and extremely low-utility offers. Their threats to walk away are cheap talk meant to force early, unbalanced concessions. I have already accommodated their preference for valueB on issueC; I will now offer valueD on issueE to demonstrate willingness to move while maintaining my utility on highly weighted issues.

The opponent is relying heavily on aggressive threats and fake urgency ("I walk", "last meaningful concession"), but their offers remain highly unfavorable to me, yielding a utility of roughly 0.37. As we are still very early in the negotiation (t=0.1), I will ignore the cheap talk and instead focus on finding mutually acceptable tradeoffs without drastically dropping my utility. I am offering a slight concession on issueB while securing high-yield values for my most critical issues (issueE and issueD).

The opponent continues to rely heavily on aggressive language and threats of deadlocking, using fake urgency and anger as a tactic. Given the early stage of negotiation (t=0.12), I will not be intimidated into accepting a significantly sub-optimal deal. However, to show willingness to find an agreement, I will adopt one of the values they demonstrated a preference for (issueD: valueC, issueC: valueB) while maintaining high-utility values on my most important issues (E and B).

The current time fraction is very early (t=0.14). The opponent is consistently sending aggressive cheap talk and threats, which I will ignore since their actual bidding behavior shows gradual concessions (e.g. moving on issueD). I will maintain a high-utility offer for myself (target ~0.73) by matching their moves on issueC (valueB) and issueD (valueC) while holding firm on my most important issues (issueE and issueB), aiming to signal that agreement requires movement on the remaining unresolved issues.

At `t=0.16`, the aggressive messages and walk-away threats from the opponent are clearly cheap talk, as there is still plenty of time remaining before the deadline. I am ignoring these fabricated constraints and focusing only on the numbers, noticing their actual concessions on issueC and issueD. To encourage further constructive movement while protecting my high-weight issues, I am making a slight concession on issueB (to valueB) and matching their position on issueD and issueC, but holding reasonably firm on issueE and issueA to maintain a solid overall utility above 0.7.

The opponent is consistently using aggressive, "walk away" tactics and fake ultimatums in their messages, which I am ignoring as cheap talk since we are only at t=0.18. Their bids have shown slight flexibility on issueD and issueE. I am targeting a utility of around 0.73 by maintaining my highest-weighted issues (D and E) at valueD, while offering a new concession on issueB to find acceptable trade-offs without sacrificing my core interests early in the negotiation.

The opponent has conceded on issueD to `valueE`, which coincidentally provides me with maximum utility (1.00) for an issue weighing 0.2. Recognizing this alignment, I will incorporate `issueD: valueE` into my bid while maintaining the opponent's preferred `issueC: valueB`. I am holding firm on my most important issues (issueE and issueB) since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.2) and I want to keep my total utility around 0.78 to 0.80. Message content is ignored as standard threat tactics.

The opponent's messages consist of empty threats and aggressive ultimatums, which I am ignoring based on the provided instructions. Through their offers at t=0.20 and t=0.22, they have revealed a willingness to accept issueD: valueE and issueE: valueC, respectively. Given that issueD is highly important to me (weight 0.2, valueE utility 1.0) and the time fraction is still low at 0.22, I will incorporate their acceptance of D: valueE while offering slight concessions on issueB, seeking a middle-ground without drastically sacrificing my total utility yet.

The opponent's repeated threats to walk away are recognizable as cheap talk and fake urgency, especially given that we are only at t = 0.24. I will ignore their aggressive rhetoric and focus entirely on the bids. They have consistently asked for issueC: valueB, which provides me with decent utility, so I will continue granting it, as well as holding issueD to valueE, which they've shown they can concede on. To make a measured concession, I will offer valueB on issueA while keeping my demands high on the heavily-weighted issues B and E.

The opponent's message history is full of empty threats about walking away, which I will ignore since we are only at t=0.26. However, they have consistently demanded valueA for issueA and valueB for issueC in every offer. Since issueA and issueC have low importance weights for me (0.1 each), I will concede entirely on issueA and maintain my concession on issueC to appease them while holding firm on my highest-priority issues (issueB, issueD, and issueE), maintaining a target total utility above 0.70.

I am still early in the negotiation (t=0.28). The opponent repeatedly sends false threats of walking away which I am disregarding as aggressive but empty cheap talk. I have decided to concede to the opponent's preferred values for my least important issues (issueA: valueA and issueC: valueB). To avoid repeating my previous offer while keeping my utility high on heavily weighted domains, I am asking for top-tier outcomes for issueB and issueD, and holding steady on issueE.

The opponent continues to combine aggressive, threatening messages with minor concessions, recently moving to valueB on issueB. Since issueA and issueC are my lowest-weighted issues, I am willing to meet their continuous demand for issueA: valueA and issueC: valueB. By also matching their concession on issueB to valueB, I am showing significant flexibility, but in return, I am holding firm on issueD and issueE to protect my highest-weighted priorities and maintain a high overall utility at this stage in the negotiation.

The opponent's aggressive threats to walk away are standard cheap talk which I will disregard, focusing solely on the math and real concessions, especially since we are at t=0.32 and have plenty of time. The opponent clearly values issueA: valueA and issueC: valueB, which are low priority for me. By conceding those, I aim to hold firm on my highest-priority issues (issueE, issueB, and issueD) to maintain utility above 0.75 while experimenting with issueB and issueE tradeoffs the opponent might accept.

The opponent's messages are highly aggressive, but their bidding behavior contradicts these statements, with successive recent concessions. They just offered `valueB` on `issueA`, so I will accept that along with their continuous bid of `valueB` on `issueC`, as both are low-weight issues for me. I am holding firm on my key issues (`issueB`, `issueD`, `issueE`), targeting a utility above 0.75, which is appropriate for `t=0.34`, while showing minor flexibility by shifting `issueD` to `valueD` (which they have previously offered at `t=0.24`).

The opponent's messages are clearly using fake urgency and aggressive posturing as tactical cheap talk, so I will rely entirely on their bidding behavior. At t=0.36, they offered `issueD: valueE`, which is my top value (utility 1.0) for this issue, signaling that a win-win tradeoff is possible. I will match their concession on `issueD` and `issueC`, while proposing a previously offered value for `issueB` (valueB) to assemble an offer that yields a strong utility of ~0.75 for me without conceding unnecessarily early on my most important issue, `issueE`.

The opponent's messages are highly repetitive and aggressive, consisting of constant but empty threats to walk away, which I am disregarding as cheap talk given the time fraction is only 0.38. I analyze their bidding pattern and see they just offered `issueA: valueA`, `issueB: valueB`, `issueC: valueB`, and `issueD: valueD`. I will match all four of these exact values to signal cooperation, but hold firm on `issueE: valueD` since issueE is my highest-weight issue, maintaining a healthy utility of 0.71 for myself during this mid-game phase.

The opponent's relentless threats to walk away are standard cheap talk which I will disregard. Numerically, they have recently conceded on issueB (valueB at t=0.38 and t=0.40) and previously on issueD (valueE at t=0.36) and issueC (valueB consistently). I will combine these previously matched values while keeping issueE high (valueD) for myself. This anchors my utility firmly above 0.7, accommodating their preferences where they have signaled flexibility without completely collapsing my overall score early in the negotiation.

The opponent's repetitive aggressive messages ("I walk", "final concession") alongside continued bidding variations indicate standard hardline haggling, so I will treat their threats as bluffs. I noticed the opponent offered `issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueD` at t=0.38 and t=0.40. I will precisely match those four values to clearly signal movement and build on common ground while holding firm on `issueE: valueD`, protecting my most important issue (weight 0.3) for a strong utility of 0.71.

The opponent's messages consist of automated, repetitive threats which I will ignore as cheap talk. They have shown consistent interest in `issueA: valueA` and `issueC: valueB`, so I will continue giving them those favorable terms on my lower-priority issues. To vary my offer without conceding too much utility at this mid-game stage, I will propose a combination of `issueD: valueD` and `issueB: valueD` alongside my preferred value for `issueE`, retaining an overall utility of ~0.72.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive, aggressive cheap talk which I will disregard. However, their latest offer finally proposes 'valueE' for issueC, which happens to be my highest utility value for that issue. By adopting 'valueE' for issueC and drawing upon their previous offers of 'valueB' for issueB and 'valueE' for issueD, I can construct an offer that integrates their proposed concessions while keeping my utility robust (target > 0.74), maintaining my strong hold on the critical issueE.

The opponent's messages are repetitive, aggressive threats designed to force rapid concessions, but their actual bids show they are slowly exploring the space (e.g., varying issueC and issueD recently). Since time is only around the halfway mark (`t=0.48`), there is no need to panic. I will concede slightly by matching their recent proposal on issueC (valueD) and easing on issueD (valueD), while holding firm on issueE (valueD) where my utility weight is the highest.

At the halfway mark (t=0.50), I recognize the hostile boilerplate messages as transparent negotiation tactics and rely entirely on the offer history. I note your recent flexibility on issueA (moving to valueB) and issueC (moving to valueD), and I have incorporated these values into my current proposal to find mutual ground. To balance this and maintain necessary utility, I am holding strong on issueD and issueE, while suggesting a middle-ground value for issueB (valueB) that you yourself have offered multiple times in recent rounds.

The negotiation is at the midway point (t=0.52). Despite the opponent's constant threats to walk away, they have continued to negotiate and just offered valueD for issueB and valueD for issueC. I am matching these values since they provide acceptable utility for me. In exchange, I am maintaining my high-utility values on my most important issues, issueD and issueE.

The opponent continues to attach aggressive "final offer" messages to their bids, but I will treat these as cheap talk since their actual bidding behavior shows ongoing concessions, such as offering my top value for issue D. I will reciprocate their movement by matching their exact latest proposed values on issues A, B, C, and D. To maintain a strong overall utility (0.728), I will hold firm on my highest-weighted issue (issue E) at valueD. This presents a highly cooperative offer that significantly bridges the gap.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive, generic walk-away threats and should be entirely ignored as cheap talk. Their last offer presented `valueE` for `issueC`, so I will incorporate that into my bid to demonstrate responsiveness while holding firm on issues `issueE` and `issueD`, which are critical to my overall utility. Time is at `t=0.56`, allowing us to continue trading across our less-valued issues without rushing to accept an unfavorable deal.

The opponent's aggressive messages threatening to walk away are consistent from the start and can be treated as cheap talk, while their actual bids show significant concessions (e.g., offering valueE on my highest-weight issueE and valueD on issueB). I will accept four out of five of their most recently proposed values (issueA, issueB, issueC, and issueE), matching their apparent preferences while anchoring issueD at valueD—which they have previously offered. This trade-off pushes us toward agreement while securing a firm utility of ~0.75 for me.

The opponent consistently threatens to walk away as a cheap talk tactic on almost every turn, which I will ignore as non-binding posturing. Looking at their recent bids, they yielded 'valueE' on my highest-weight issueE at roughly t=0.58, and frequently proposed 'valueB' for issueB, 'valueD' for issueC, and 'valueC' for issueD (such as at t=0.48). Combining these historic offers into a single package allows me to signal cooperation and concede down to roughly ~0.70 utility, while still protecting maximum utility on my most heavily weighted issue.

The opponent's messaging remains aggressive, yet their actual proposals show movement, as seen in their recent shift to valueB for issueA and valueD for issueB at t=0.62. To encourage a deal while protecting my priorities, I will adopt their latest acceptable values for issueA, issueB, and issueC. In exchange, I am holding firm on my highest-priority issues, issueD and issueE, selecting values they have offered in previous turns (valueE for both).

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive, identical empty threats to walk away, which I am disregarding as a cheap-talk tactic. Observing their recent offers, they appear to favor `valueB` for `issueA` and `valueE` for `issueC`, while trying to pull `issueD` towards `valueC` and `issueE` towards `valueA`. I will accommodate them on `issueA` and `issueC` using values they recently proposed, but maintain a firm stance on my heavily weighted issues (`issueE` and `issueD`) to secure a favorable utility above 0.75.

I noticed the opponent finally offered `issueB: valueE`, which is my top value for that issue, while maintaining their strong preference for `issueA: valueA`. I will accept their proposal on `issueA` and `issueB`. To further build consensus, I am including `issueC: valueB`, which they have requested extensively, while holding firm on `issueD` and `issueE` to keep my utility target around 0.79 as we enter the later stages of negotiation.

The opponent's repetitive, aggressive messages are boilerplate negotiation tactics and can be ignored as cheap talk, especially since their actual bids show continued flexibility. To find a mutually acceptable agreement as time progresses to 0.68, I am adopting three of the exact values they proposed in their last offer (issueA: valueB, issueB: valueB, and issueC: valueD). I will hold firm on issueD and issueE to protect my core utility, remaining safely above my reservation value with an overall utility of approximately 0.76.

The current time is 0.7, meaning we are approaching the late stages of negotiation. The opponent's messages are extremely repetitive cheap talk filled with empty threats and fabricated urgency, so I will ignore the text and rely entirely on their actual bids. They recently offered `valueD` for `issueA` and frequently use `valueB` for `issueC` and `issueB`. I will concede on those three issues by matching those values, while retaining my high-utility values on my heavily weighted issues, `issueD` and `issueE`. This guarantees my total utility remains around 0.80 while coming closer to the opponent's historical proposals.

The opponent has shown a strong preference for `valueA` on `issueA`, so I will fully concede on that issue to build goodwill, as it has low importance for me. In return, I will accept their generous offers of `valueE` on both `issueC` and `issueD`, which grant me excellent utility. I will hold slightly firmer on `issueB` and `issueE` by proposing `valueD`, targeting a solid overall utility while matching elements from their recent offers as the deadline approaches.

The opponent's aggression in their messages is persistent but largely cheap talk; nevertheless, I am paying close attention to their offers which are showing variable movement. At t=0.72, they proposed a combination (valueA, valueC, valueE, valueE, valueC) that is highly acceptable on most issues except issueE, which is my most important priority. To seek agreement, I will match four of their t=0.72 values (issueA, issueB, issueC, issueD) while holding firm near my top preferences for issueE (valueD) to maintain my utility around 0.70.

The opponent's repeated claims of "final offer" and threats to walk away are transparent negotiation tactics, since they have continued to concede on various issues (e.g., offering valueE for issueC and valueC for issueB). To accommodate their latest movement, I will accept their most recent values for issues A, B, and C (valueB, valueC, and valueE respectively), which they just proposed at t=0.76. By conceding on these lower-weight issues, I can firmly hold onto high values for my most critical issues, D and E.

The opponent continues to use aggressive framing and fake walk-away threats, which I safely treat as cheap talk. Their latest offer at t=0.78, however, includes highly favorable positions for me across multiple issues, specifically issueC (valueE) and issueD (valueD). I will match their exact bids on issues A, B, C, and D to signal readiness for an agreement, while holding firm only on my most important issue (issueE) by offering valueD. This maintains my overall utility at a strong 0.75 while presenting a nearly identical package to their last offer.

The opponent's most recent offer at t=0.80 provides a strong utility of 0.74 to me, as they conceded significantly on my highest-weighted issues (B, D, and E). Given time pressure is increasing, we will closely match their offer but adjust issue C to valueE—a value they have recently offered multiple times (e.g. at t=0.72, 0.76, 0.78), attempting to maximize utility to ≈0.75 while remaining highly agreeable.

The negotiation is in the late stages (t=0.82), so I am moving closer to the opponent's demands to secure an agreement. The opponent has repeatedly sent "final offer" threats, which I recognize as cheap talk, but I also see convergence in the actual offers on issues A, B, C, and D. I will exactly match the opponent's most recent requests on issues A, B, C, and D (valueB, valueB, valueD, and valueD respectively), while holding firm on my highest-weight issue, E, at valueD to protect my overall utility.

The opponent's most recent offer dropped significantly in utility, but they recently offered a highly favorable package at t=0.80 and t=0.82 (including valueD for issues B, C, D, and E). Since they also just signaled willingness to accept valueE for issueA at t=0.84, I will merge these signals. This keeps my utility above 0.80 while offering a combination of values they have explicitly proposed within the last few rounds.

The deadline is imminent at t=0.86, and although your recent messages have been aggressive, your bidding history reveals workable compromises. Specifically, at t=0.80, you proposed a combination that aligns well with my core interests while granting you your preferred value on issueA. I am formally retreating to and adopting your exact t=0.80 offer to secure a mutually beneficial agreement before time expires.
